response: Overfitting occurs when a model performs well on the training data but poorly on new, unseen data, resulting from the model being too closely tied to the specific characteristics of the training set.
reference: Overfitting occurs when a model performs well on training data but generalizes poorly to new unseen data. It happens when the model is too complex relative to the amount and noisiness of the training data.

response:
Cross-validation is a technique used to evaluate the performance of a machine learning model by training and testing it on multiple subsets of the available data. This helps to avoid overfitting, which occurs when a model becomes too specialized to the training data.

You would prefer cross-validation to using a single validation set because it allows you to estimate the model's performance more accurately on unseen data. By dividing the data into multiple folds and performing k-fold cross-validation (k=5 is commonly used), you can train and test your model on each fold in turn, which helps to capture the variability in the data. This results in a more robust estimate of the model's performance than using just one validation set.

Cross-validation would likely lead you to converge as well because it allows you to tune hyperparameters on multiple subsets of the data, which can help to avoid overfitting and ensure that the best parameters are selected for your specific problem.
reference: Cross-validation is a technique to evaluate model performance by splitting data into multiple folds, training on some folds and validating on the remaining fold, rotating until every fold has been used for validation.
